highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.183: Auto Mode Now Blocks Dangerous Git Commands
highlights.summary: The latest release adds important safety guardrails in auto mode, blocking destructive git commands like `git reset --hard` and `git clean -fd` when you haven't explicitly asked to discard local work. It also prevents `git commit --amend` on commits not made by the agent in the current session, protecting your history from unintended rewrites. This is a significant quality-of-life improvement for developers who run Claude Code autonomously on real codebases.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.181: Set Config Values Directly from the Prompt
highlights.summary: You can now use `/config key=value` syntax directly in the Claude Code prompt to change settings on the fly ��� for example, `/config thinking=false` to disable extended thinking without leaving the terminal. This works in interactive mode, headless `-p` mode, and Remote Control, making it much easier to experiment with settings mid-session. Beginners will find this especially handy for quickly toggling behaviors without hunting through config files.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.178: Fine-Grained Permission Rules with Tool Parameter Matching
highlights.summary: A new `Tool(param:value)` syntax lets you write permission rules that match on a tool's input parameters, including wildcard support. A practical example: use `Agent(model:opus)` to block Claude from spinning up expensive Opus subagents without your approval. Skills in nested `.claude/skills` directories also now load contextually, with deeper skills taking priority on name clashes.

highlights.title: Anthropic Pauses Credit Change for Claude Code Headless/SDK Usage
highlights.summary: Anthropic has postponed a previously announced billing change that would have moved `claude -p`, the Agent SDK, and third-party apps off subscription rate limits onto a separate monthly credit pool. If you rely on headless or programmatic Claude Code usage, your current billing setup remains unchanged for now. Keep an eye on official communications for when this change is rescheduled, as it will affect cost planning for automated workflows.

highlights.title: Show HN: Pulse – Approve Claude Code Tool Calls from Your Phone
highlights.summary: Pulse is a community-built local dashboard that lets you review and approve Claude Code tool calls remotely from your phone, useful when you start a long agentic run and step away from your desk. It's a lightweight install built by a student developer and reflects a growing ecosystem of tools extending Claude Code's supervision capabilities. If you frequently run Claude Code in auto mode, this kind of remote oversight tool can help you stay in control without babysitting your terminal.

highlights.title: From Zero to Claude Code in 19 Minutes (No Code)
highlights.summary: This beginner-friendly YouTube tutorial walks through getting Claude Code up and running from scratch in under 20 minutes, with no prior coding experience required. It's a great starting point if you're new to AI coding assistants and want a quick, practical introduction before diving into longer courses. Watch this first if you haven't yet installed or used Claude Code at all.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: This concise YouTube tutorial is aimed at developers who want a solid practical foundation in Claude Code without committing to a multi-hour course. It covers core workflows and likely includes tips on effective prompting and tool use within a tight 30-minute format. A good middle-ground option if you already know the basics and want to level up your day-to-day usage.

tips_of_the_day: Before starting a long autonomous Claude Code session, add permission rules like `Agent(model:opus)` to your settings to prevent unexpected expensive subagent calls — your wallet will thank you.
Use the new `/config thinking=false` slash command mid-session to quickly disable extended thinking when you need faster, cheaper responses for simple tasks, then re-enable it for complex reasoning.

Always commit (or at least stash) your local changes before running Claude Code in auto mode — even with the new git safety guardrails in v2.1.183, a clean working tree is your best protection against unintended modifications.
